title: CodeRs workshop: Scaling up with R and Katana
text:
Access the GitHub repo for this workshop.


https://github.com/kristenbhunter/codeRs_katana_workshop


Windows users: Download putty.


https://www.chiark.greenend.org.uk/~sgtatham/putty/latest.html


Download FileZilla.


https://filezilla-project.org/


Bonus: Check if you can log in to Katana.


We will also do this during the workshop!
https://docs.restech.unsw.edu.au/using_katana/accessing_katana/

title: CodeRs workshop: Scaling up with R and Katana
text:
Best subset selection — identifying the optimal \(k\) predictors from \(p\) candidates — is fundamental for building interpretable and parsimonious statistical models, but the underlying combinatorial problem is NP-hard.
COMBSS (Continuous Optimisation for Best Subset Selection) overcomes this barrier by reformulating the discrete problem as a continuous optimisation, making it scalable to high-dimensional settings where \(p\) far exceeds \(n\).
This presentation walks through the framework for linear, logistic, and multinomial regression, illustrates its performance on simulated data and two real biomedical applications — cancer gene-expression classification (\(p = 2{,}308\) genes) and a rice GWAS (\(p \approx 158{,}000\) SNPs) — and demonstrates the open-source combss (R and Python) packages with practical examples.

title: CodeRs workshop: Scaling up with R and Katana
text:
How this demonstration is structured
Each section below has its own page; navigate via the top menu or click through in order.



Section
Pages




Motivation — what sparse-constrained GLM is, and why MIO / lasso are not the last word
Sparse GLM → MIO → Lasso → COMBSS (our method)


Methodology — Boolean relaxation and the homotopy Frank-Wolfe algorithm
Relaxation → Homotopy Frank-Wolfe


Demos — five runnable demos in R and Python
Linear sim · HD logistic · Khan SRBCT · Rice GWAS · Comparisons


Use it yourself
Install · References

title: CodeRs workshop: Scaling up with R and Katana
text:
Packages

R — combss on CRAN — install.packages("combss")
Python — combss on PyPI — pip install combss

title: Logging in
text:
I will only be discussing accessing katana via terminal.
You can also access through Katana OnDemand, a graphical session where you can access Rstudio
To access Katana via terminal, we will use SSH (Secure SHell) from your local machine.



Open up Terminal and SSH into katana:




First you will need to download a SSH client such as Putty.

title: Shell commands
text:
If you haven’t used a shell before to access a computer, it can be intimidating! But knowing just a few commands can get you quite far.
Also known as bash, unix, or linux commands.

title: Shell commands
text:
If you haven’t used a shell before to access a computer, it can be intimidating! But knowing just a few commands can get you quite far.
Also known as bash, unix, or linux commands.

title: Shell commands
text:
Navigate

cd: change directory
ls: list
pwd: print working directory

title: Shell commands
text:
File management

mkdir: make a directory
rmdir: remove a directory
cp: copy files
mv: move files
rm: remove files

title: Shell commands
text:
Recursion

Adding -r makes the command recursive, which means it will apply to all files in a directory, not just one at a time.
Make sure you know what you are doing! rm -r deletes a whole directory!

title: Transferring files
text:
Option 1: Use a file transfer program

This option is probably best for most people.
Katana recommends FileZilla.

Option 2: Use git

Requires command-line knowledge of git.
Ensures consistency of file versions across machines.
No need to remember if the file on katana is the latest version!
I will not cover this option, but feel free to ask me about it.

title: Running a job
text: Katana uses a job management system called OpenPBS (PBS = portable batch system). A job is a computational task, such as running an R script. You use PBS to run jobs, stop jobs, and check on their status.

title: Running a job
text: Katana uses a job management system called OpenPBS (PBS = portable batch system). A job is a computational task, such as running an R script. You use PBS to run jobs, stop jobs, and check on their status.

title: Running a job
text:
Set up your pbs script
The following is a basic pbs script, scripts/run.pbs.
#!/bin/bash

#PBS -N coders
#PBS -l select=1:ncpus=1:mem=4gb
#PBS -l walltime=1:00:00
#PBS -M email@unsw.edu.au
#PBS -m abe

module load r/4.5.1

Rscript /home/z0000000/test_script.R

Tasks

Open scripts/run.pbs in Rstudio.
Change the email address to your personal email address.
Change the Rscript directory to your zID.
Transfer this script to your coders directory on katana using FileZilla.

title: Running a job
text:
Start a job
Run the following:
qsub run.pbs
The following should happen:

The terminal prints out the job id 0000.kman.restech.unsw.edu.au
Once the job starts (might take a couple of minutes):

You should receive an email that the job has started.
Type ls and you will see two files:

coders.e8181993: messages and errors.
coders.o8181993: output, like print statements.
These will continue to get updated as the job runs.
Try running cat on both these files to see their contents.

title: Running a job
text:
Job management

Job status
To check on all your jobs, run:
qstat -su z0000000
where the number is your zID.
This will only show currently-running jobs–as soon as the job completes, it will not show up!


Delete a job
To stop a job, run:
qdel 00000
where the number is the job ID.

title: Running a job
text:
Advanced commands
Want to explore what options there are for running jobs? Type
man qsub
to show you the manual. Press q to quit
All of these flags can included in one of two ways:

When you run the job qsub -N coders
At the top of the .pbs script #PBS -N coders
Note: -N adds a job name.

title: Shell commands
text:
Seeing and editing files

cat prints out the contents of a file.
nano is a simple text editor that allows you to edit files on the terminal. There are other options, but I find this is the most user-friendly because it has instructions at the bottom. Running nano filename will start the nano editor.

title: CodeRs workshop: Scaling up with R and Katana
text:
I will spend very little time on understanding R code itself, and assume you already have beginner/intermediate R knowledge.
I assume you have little or no experience with Katana.
If there is enough interest we may offer a future workshop for more advanced users, including running large numbers of jobs and advanced job management. Let me know!
The beginning of this workshop overlaps a lot with general, non-R focused Katana materials. Toward the end we will get into some information that is more specific to R users.

title: Interactive R session
text:
Run module avail to see the extensive list of software pre-installed on Katana.
The following R versions are available

title: Interactive R session
text:
Run module avail to see the extensive list of software pre-installed on Katana.
The following R versions are available

title: Interactive R session
text:
Run interactive R
Run the following commands:

module load r/4.5.1
R

Now you are running R interactively! Within R, run:

Run install.packages("palmerpenguins")
Run install.packages("ggplot2")
Quit by typing q()
Do not save workspace image.

Notes on using interactive R:

I rarely use this feature!
Before running R, you have to run module load every time you log into Katana. You only have to run it once per session.
I use it help me test whether a script will run, mostly to see if files are in the right place and libraries are installed.
I also use this to install the necessary R libraries before running jobs, so I don’t need to re-install packages every time I run a script.



Next: Running a job →

title: Passing values
text:
We are going to run a slightly more complicated script that creates a plot and saves it to a png file.


Modify the following scripts with your personal details/file paths:

scripts/create_plot1.R
scripts/create_plot2.R
scripts/run_colour.pbs

Next:

Transfer all the above files to your Katana coders directory.
Modify run.pbs to run create_plot1.R instead of test_script.R

You can modify it on your local machine and transfer it.
Or, you can use nano (or your preferred text editor) in the terminal to edit it directly on Katana.

title: Passing values
text:
Run the script

Run qsub run.pbs
Once the job has run, run ls to see if plot.png has appeared.
Transfer plot.png back to your local machine to see if it worked.

The current plot has the points coloured in UNSW yellow. We are going to try to pass in a different colour from the terminal into our R script.

title: Passing values
section: Passing in a value to the R script
text:
Passing in a value to the R script

Setting the environment variable
Let’s take a look at run_colour.pbs
#!/bin/bash

#PBS -N coders
#PBS -l select=1:ncpus=1:mem=4gb
#PBS -l walltime=1:00:00
#PBS -M email@unsw.edu.au
#PBS -m abe

module load r/4.5.1

colour="#2a9d8f"
export colour

Rscript /home/z0000000/coders/create_plot2.R
What is going on here?
colour="#2a9d8f"
export colour

We set an environment variable called “colour”.
We export the variable. This makes sure the variable is accessible to processes outside of the bash script we are running.

Try running the above two commands just in your terminal. Then run the following to see the value of “colour”:
echo $colour


Pulling in the value to the R script
Now let’s take a look at create_plot2.R.
The following line extracts the “colour” variable from the environment variables.
point.colour <- Sys.getenv("colour")

title: Passing values
text:
Check that it works!

Pick a new colour for the points on the graph and find the hex code for it.

HTML Color Picker

Modify run_colour.pbs with your hex code.
Run the job.
After the job finishes, transfer plot.png back to your machine.
Did it work?

title: Loops
text:
We are going to run a simulation. It simulates data, conducts a t test, and saves the p-value. We want to pass several different values into this simulation in a loop.


Modify the following scripts with your personal details/file paths:

scripts/simulation.R
scripts/run_sim.pbs

And transfer them to Katana.

title: Loops
text:
We are going to run a simulation. It simulates data, conducts a t test, and saves the p-value. We want to pass several different values into this simulation in a loop.


Modify the following scripts with your personal details/file paths:

scripts/simulation.R
scripts/run_sim.pbs

And transfer them to Katana.

title: Loops
text:
Run a loop
Paste the following into your terminal. It is also available in scripts/loop.txt.
nList=("10" "50" "100")

for i in ${nList[@]}; do
    
  # assign vars
  n=$i
  echo $n

  # Run R script
  qsub -v n=$n run_sim.pbs

  # pause to be kind to the scheduler and to get unique run IDs
  sleep 10

done
What is the purpose of -v n=$n for in qsub -V run_sim.pbs? This is essential! It passes $n so it is available to the job.

Check the results
Do you have a 3 files called pval_20260406_00000.RDS in your directory? If yes, it worked!


Next: Misc Advice →

Sign up for the codeRs mentorship program!
This is a 10-week, flexible program, allowing the mentee and mentor to decide how regularly they would like to catch-up. If you are interested in being a mentee, please use this form to express your interest. You can sign up to be a mentor, mentee, or both!
https://unsw-coders.netlify.app/mentoring-program.html

title: A collection of miscellaneous advice
text:
Regardless of whether you are conducting a project locally or on Katana, I always like to create two directories:

in: contains any input data. I never write to this directory!
out: contains any output from jobs, such as RDS files, images, etc.

title: A collection of miscellaneous advice
text:
Regardless of whether you are conducting a project locally or on Katana, I always like to create two directories:

in: contains any input data. I never write to this directory!
out: contains any output from jobs, such as RDS files, images, etc.

title: A collection of miscellaneous advice
text:
Log files
I find it very annoying that all the output and error files clutter up my project directory.
I like to create a folder called log and set the following options at the top of my PBS file:
#PBS -o log/
#PBS -e log/
This sends the output and error files to the log directory.

title: A collection of miscellaneous advice
text:
Paths

here package
It is annoying to re-set paths when you switch back and forth between your local machine and the cloud. Something that helps is using the “here” package. It helps set paths relative to “here”.

here::here()

[1] "/Users/z3535929/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-UNSW/kemps/codeRs_katana_workshop"



here::here("out", "pval.RDS")

[1] "/Users/z3535929/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-UNSW/kemps/codeRs_katana_workshop/out/pval.RDS"




Toggle variable
Alternatively, sometimes I set a clunky option: I have a single variable called local which sets a bunch of variable values depending on whether I am on my local machine or in the cloud.
local <- TRUE
if(local)
{
  # local
  codedir <- here::here()
  outdirbase <- here::here()
} else
{
  # cloud
  codedir <- Sys.getenv("codedir")
  outdirbase <- Sys.getenv("outdirbase")
}
